death.cause: Hanged as a spy by British forces without trial; he was not in uniform.

burial.cemetery_name: Artillery Park (initial) — later moved to Nathan Hale Cemetery Coventry CT
burial.plot_notes: Body left to hang for days as warning at British headquarters Artillery Park at approximately 66th Street and Third Avenue Manhattan. A British soldier removed a painted soldier from a rebel gentleman's garden and hung it alongside the body labeled 'George Washington' — a letter from a British officer in the Kentish Gazette November 1776 records this. The body was eventually removed; cenotaph and family marker at Nathan Hale Cemetery Coventry CT.

parents.father_research_notes: Deacon Richard Hale (1717-1802) of Coventry CT. Younger son Nathan never married.
parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Strong Hale of Northampton MA.

education.notes: Yale College B.A. 1773 — prepared for college by Rev Joseph Huntington (Y00008) of Coventry.

career.notes: Schoolmaster at New London CT after Yale graduation, before joining Continental Army.

military_service.notes: Volunteered to gather intelligence behind British lines on Long Island, September 1776. Captured returning from the mission. Hanged at New York September 22, 1776 without trial as a spy (not in uniform). Final witnessed words per British Captain Frederick MacKenzie's diary: 'He behaved with great composure and resolution saying he thought it the duty of every good officer to obey any orders given to him by his commander in chief; and desired the spectators to be all times prepared to meet death in whatever shape it may appear.' The popularly known 'I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country' tradition dates from later accounts.

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army spy hanged by British, 1776

cross_connections.display_label: Prepared him for Yale at the Coventry parsonage
Niece who married into the Connecticut Hooker family, 1811
Nephew; physician who married twice into the Hooker family
Yale classmate who directed the Culper Spy Ring

narrative_blocks.header: British hanged him as spy at New York
narrative_blocks.body: Captured behind British lines on Long Island while gathering intelligence for George Washington, and hanged without trial at Artillery Park New York City September 22, 1776, age 21. Gen William Howe noted in his diary: 'A spy from the enemy (by his own full confession) apprehended last night, was this day executed at 11 o'clock in front of Artillery Park.'

narrative_blocks.header: Body left to hang for days as warning
narrative_blocks.body: The British left the body to hang as a warning to other rebel spies, with a painted soldier removed from a rebel gentleman's garden hung alongside labeled 'George Washington' — recorded in a British officer's letter published in the Kentish Gazette November 1776. The execution site at British Artillery Park stood at roughly 66th Street and Third Avenue in present-day Manhattan.

narrative_blocks.header: Tutored for Yale by Coventry minister Joseph Huntington
narrative_blocks.body: Prepared for Yale entrance by Rev Joseph Huntington of Coventry — the same minister whose posthumous Universalist treatise his daughter would burn. Hale entered Yale 1769 at age 14, graduated 1773, briefly taught school at New London CT, and joined Knowlton's Rangers when the war began.

narrative_blocks.header: Famous last words recorded by witness MacKenzie
narrative_blocks.body: British Captain Frederick MacKenzie wrote in his diary September 22, 1776 that Hale 'behaved with great composure and resolution' and 'desired the spectators to be at all times prepared to meet death in whatever shape it may appear.' The popularly remembered 'I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country' comes from later accounts and may paraphrase a Cato-echoing sentiment Hale was likely to have voiced.

The body was eventually removed; cenotaph and family marker at Nathan Hale Cemetery Coventry CT.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
